title: Masculine Protest
wikitext:
[]: "Masculine Protest"?             Quote Post by Veoren » Sun Nov 10, 2013 2:28 am [Title corrected. - Monk] When Asuka is being mentally probed by Arael, one of the phrases near the end is "Masculine Priest"? Am I seeing things? I get all the other ones "Father" "Mother" "I'm not a doll", etc.. But WTH with this one? Anyone have any ideas? Does it really say that? I cant seem to pause the video at JUST the right moment.. Here is a link, look towards the very end: http://www.youtube.com/watch?v=VJ0doO3VFjc

[]:             Quote Post by The Killer of Heroes » Sun Nov 10, 2013 2:34 am It says "Masculine Protest". SPOILER: Show

[]:             Quote Post by Veoren » Sun Nov 10, 2013 2:36 am Ahhh! Thank you so much! Well now I'm even more confused though.. WHAT does this mean? And WHAT does it have to do with poor little Asuka?

[]:             Quote Post by The Killer of Heroes » Sun Nov 10, 2013 2:40 am I think you could read it as either meaning "Asuka protests the masculine/male characters" (Perhaps referring to how she wants Shinji to be more "manly".) or "Asuka protests in a masculine way" (AKA speaking her mind, generally being a little rough etc.). Both apply to the character.

[]:             Quote Post by Veoren » Sun Nov 10, 2013 9:39 pm The Killer of Heroes wrote: I think you could read it as either meaning "Asuka protests the masculine/male characters" (Perhaps referring to how she wants Shinji to be more "manly".) or "Asuka protests in a masculine way" (AKA speaking her mind, generally being a little rough etc.). Both apply to the character.

[]:             Quote Post by Fireball » Mon Nov 11, 2013 1:38 am Veoren wrote: Well now I'm even more confused though.. WHAT does this mean? And WHAT does it have to do with poor little Asuka?

[]:             Quote Post by The Killer of Heroes » Mon Nov 11, 2013 2:55 am Well it looks like I was way off.

[]:             Quote Post by Kendrix » Mon Nov 11, 2013 7:07 am As she tells us herself in episode 25, her old man appears to have permanently ruined her opinion of the penis-owning kind...

[]:             Quote Post by Veoren » Tue Dec 17, 2013 12:01 pm Fireball wrote: http://www.encyclopedia69.com/eng/d/masculine-protest/masculine-protest.htm

[]:             Quote Post by Bagheera » Tue Dec 17, 2013 3:22 pm Veoren wrote: So, if Asuka had been male, she would be all "passive" instead? I dunno, this all seems a little sexist.

[]:             Quote Post by SoryuUberAlles » Wed Dec 18, 2013 2:53 pm But, like, being super-neurotic and technical, Asuka as a woman cannot suffer from "masculine protest" anymore than Shinji can suffer from penis envy, right? A quick reminder that Evangelion's psychological sources are actually quite a bit heterogeneous, from Klein, to Freud to Adler and I think just a soupcon of Lacan. I feel better knowing that, last Freud I read "Introjection" (ep. 19) is actually supposed to occur during "Oral Stage" (ep. 20). Potential sense-making FTW!

[]:             Quote Post by sulla » Wed Apr 09, 2014 4:08 pm Bagheera wrote: The opposite, I think. The idea is that both responses are rejections of assigned gender norms, responses made by people who don't fit in with society's expectations of them. So Asuka becomes aggressive because she's expected to be a passive female, and a male would become passive because he's expected to be an aggressive male. Both are saying "no, I don't want that, stop telling me how to behave!" It's not "this is how women are" or "this is how women ought to be" so much as "this is what people do when society tells them this is how they are or how they ought to be." IOW it's descriptive, not proscriptive.

[]:             Quote Post by Gob Hobblin » Wed Apr 09, 2014 4:16 pm Gender role is something that (especially at the time) is more stringently defined in Japanese culture. Women are expected to be more passive there, in a traditional sense. Arguably, all cultures have 'female passivity' as something that is expected of women. Equal gender roles is something I would say has really only gotten traction in the last decade, proven by the fact that it is still a far cry from anything that can remotely be considered equal. It does bring up the interesting question of what Asuka's character says about gender roles, especially in how she behaves (which is all over the map, from being flirtatious to being 'masculine'). Look at all the jokes about how Shinji is the girl and Asuka wears the pants.

[]:             Quote Post by sulla » Wed Apr 09, 2014 4:25 pm Gob Hobblin wrote: Gender role is something that (especially at the time) is more stringently defined in Japanese culture. Women are expected to be more passive there, in a traditional sense. Arguably, all cultures have 'female passivity' as something that is expected of women. Equal gender roles is something I would say has really only gotten traction in the last decade, proven by the fact that it is still a far cry from anything that can remotely be considered equal. It does bring up the interesting question of what Asuka's character says about gender roles, especially in how she behaves (which is all over the map, from being flirtatious to being 'masculine'). Look at all the jokes about how Shinji is the girl and Asuka wears the pants.

[]:             Quote Post by Gob Hobblin » Wed Apr 09, 2014 4:38 pm ^ .....huh? Where are you living that women have the advantage over men? I mean, here in the States, there is wage disparity, continued arguments over the rights women have to their own reproduction...hell, I can give you a list of everything in the MILITARY that demonstrates the sheer disparity women there suffer (being in the military myself, I can attest to a lot of that). And what does it matter where Asuka grew up? She was in Japan. And yes, Germany also has to deal with gender-roles: every Western culture deals with gender roles, just as Eastern cultures do. Germany is no exception. ...I am still really confused by your statement concerning 'social dominance.' I literally do not see any situation in society where women have some sort of social dominance over men, or men being victims of 'supplication' at the roles of women. Where did that come from? What evidence do you have to support that assertion? That's getting off topic, that statement really threw me off the tracks.

[]:             Quote Post by Bagheera » Wed Apr 09, 2014 4:51 pm Gob Hobblin wrote: ...I am still really confused by your statement concerning 'social dominance.' I literally do not see any situation in society where women have some sort of social dominance over men, or men being victims of 'supplication' at the roles of women. Where did that come from? What evidence do you have to support that assertion? That's getting off topic, that statement really threw me off the tracks.

[]:             Quote Post by Gob Hobblin » Wed Apr 09, 2014 4:55 pm ^ ....but it's...so...hard... not to ........

[]:             Quote Post by Bagheera » Wed Apr 09, 2014 4:59 pm Gob Hobblin wrote: ^ ....but it's...so...hard... not to ........

[]:             Quote Post by Gob Hobblin » Wed Apr 09, 2014 5:04 pm Touche, Bagheera. You are correct, as you tend to be frequently.

[]:             Quote Post by Bagheera » Wed Apr 09, 2014 5:12 pm Gob Hobblin wrote: Touche, Bagheera. You are correct, as you tend to be frequently.
